text: I want to make a set of modified Edison batteries with graphene. I’m looking into using either foam/plastic substrates imbedded with metal oxides or fine iron & nickel mesh. Would your conductive graphene ink be compatible with one or both applications and will it stand up to sodium or potassium hydroxides? Thanks for your input...

author: @d.l4851
text:
Hello Robert. I've been following your work for some time and it seems like a brilliant job, thank you.

Have you experimented with the shungite mineral? I think it is very useful in superconductive painting as well as in graphene batteries in a variable percentage to alter conductivity and current flow.

I hope I can help along the way!

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: javier ligas Positive Thermal Coefficient. In my experience, Robert never shows us things that are particularly difficult to make, but neither does he give you all of the answers in one video. There are hundreds of videos on his YouTube channel that give information that will take you 85% of the way. The rest will be down to your own experimentation and by reading the huge library of scientific articles on Google Scholar. Once you start experimenting and putting videos on YouTube yourself, don't be surprised if Robert gives you a little advice from time to time on the things that you should be looking at to improve your work.

author: @RWBHere
text:
Some more potential applications for that ink, in no particular order:


Seed germination.
Electric blankets.
Food warmers.
Drink warmers.
Foot warmers.
Hand warming gloves for motorcyclists, etc.
Post natal care of babies in incubators.
Under floor heating.
Wall heating.
Paint curing.
Adhesive curing.
Culture-growing in labs.
Regulating heat exchangers.
Intruder detectors.
Ovens for temperature-controlled crystal oscillators.
Greenhouse temperature regulation.
Therapeutic heat pads. (I use one of those, at night, but conventional ones can easily become too warm.)
Anti-condensation systems.
Car seats.
Car steering wheel heaters.
Traffic counting.
Traffic speed monitors.
Traffic light vehicle detectors.
Pedestrian crossing monitors.

The list goes on. I call that 'Awesome, mate!'. Thanks Robert.

author: @Miata822
text:
"Don't look for recipes and pat answer, look for solving problems"
Exactly right. I built a business around that philosophy.  It's also important to choose your projects wisely. Not everything that CAN be done, SHOULD be done, at least in a business environment. I look at an industry and ask myself; Where is an unmet need or an existing solution that is universally used but consistently inadequate. It's surprising how many opportunities are out there.

I just now discovered this channel. I will be following it.

replies.author: @mouldykev
replies.text:
Vino Veritas yes mate i did hear Rob say that but i was specifically reffering to a thermistor 😎 when i said it not a heat mat.
But just another thought, what if this tech could be incorporated into battery carbons and have a self regulating charge ! But am i thinking of the impossible ?
Either way if possible you would not be able to overcharge a battery... wow

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: kev wallcroft There's massive potential with all of these carbon inks, but without creating an open source project I don't think we'll ever achieve 1% of what could be possible. I would like to see an open source recipe that can be used to create Energy Storage Devices. I think too much emphasis is placed on trying to perform as good as, if not better than, current lithium tech. I personally don't see lithium as a relevant technology in terms of creating ESDs for the greater good of humanity and trying to halt the destruction of our planet. I have watched so many of Rob's videos, yours, Cayrex2's, Ehochimalpi's, etc... and it's obvious that there's a recipe for an ink that you all could agree on that could be used for the basis of open source ESDs. Solar PV coupled with these open source ESDs would be revolutionary.

replies.author: @dredrotten
replies.text: It sounds OK to me? A bit boomy from the echoes off the walls but who cares? What I dont like is that forensic type of audio where you can hear every click of a tongue on the mucous in someones mouth as they are speaking.Yuk!!!  Ultra Hi Fidelity should be reserved for music, movies, etc...  A lot of research has gone into microphones/audio on video cameras, so theyve had to compromise with the audio to please as many people as possible. As I said, this sounds good to me!
